result:
:::writing
The first time the city forgot my name, I thought it was a joke.

I was standing at the corner of Delancey and Norfolk, waiting for the light to change, when the crossing signal chirped and a voice I’d never heard before said, “You may cross, pedestrian.” It used to say my name. Not out loud—nothing so dramatic—but in the subtle way things align when they recognize you. The traffic always seemed to hesitate just a second longer for me, the gusts of wind paused as I stepped off the curb, and the taxis—impatient, hungry taxis—never lunged quite as close.

But that day, the wind shoved me forward like I was late, and a cab skimmed my elbow hard enough that I felt the shape of it for minutes after.

I checked my pockets. Wallet, keys, phone. Everything was there. Still, something had been misplaced.

Inside the bodega, the man behind the counter didn’t nod. He didn’t not nod—he just looked through me like I was a pane of slightly smudged glass. I picked up my usual coffee, black and bitter, and set it down with exact change. He didn’t say thank you. The receipt printer whirred but produced nothing.

“You didn’t print—” I started.

He blinked. “Print what?”

“Never mind.”

Outside, I tried to remember when the city had first learned my name. It wasn’t like a formal introduction. There had been no ceremony, no contract signed beneath fluorescent lights. It was gradual, like becoming known to a dog that no longer barked when you approached. The subway doors used to hold a fraction longer when I sprinted down the stairs. The streetlights flicked green just as I arrived. Once, a newspaper box popped open without a coin, just as I reached for it, like it knew I’d pay later.

I had always paid later.

By afternoon, the forgetting had spread. My phone stopped autocorrecting my name. My email address appeared as a string of letters I didn’t recognize. When I called my sister, she answered with cautious politeness, like I was a colleague she couldn’t quite place.

“Who is this?” she asked.

“It’s me.”

A pause. “I’m sorry, I think you have the wrong number.”

“I don’t,” I said, but I could hear how thin it sounded.

After she hung up, I checked my contacts. Her name wasn’t there. There were numbers, dozens of them, but each labeled with a blank space, like someone had gone through with a careful eraser and removed the identifying parts while leaving everything else intact.

I went home—at least, I went to the apartment I believed was mine. The key fit, but the lock resisted, as if it had developed a small, stubborn will. Inside, the furniture was arranged slightly differently. Not enough to be obvious, but enough to suggest a different life had been lived here, one with preferences just adjacent to mine.

There was a photograph on the wall I didn’t recognize: a group of people at a beach, sunburned and laughing. I stood among them, but my face was blurred—not by motion, but by absence, like the camera had refused to record me fully.

I sat on the couch and tried to say my name out loud.

Nothing came out.

Not silence—there was sound, a shape of a word—but it slipped away before I could hear it. Like trying to recall a dream that dissolves as you describe it.

“Okay,” I said to the empty room, which seemed less empty than before, as if it were occupied by all the versions of me that had ever sat here and were now politely stepping aside. “Okay.”

There are rules to these things. Not written rules, not posted anywhere, but felt. The city forgetting you wasn’t random. Cities are too busy for randomness. It had to be earned, or neglected, or both.

I tried to inventory my recent life. Work, sleep, errands. I had become efficient in the way a machine is efficient—no wasted motion, no unnecessary interaction. I had stopped noticing the barista’s haircut, stopped asking the doorman about his daughter’s recital, stopped looking up at the sky between buildings because I already knew it would be there.

I had, in small, precise ways, stopped participating.

That night, I walked.

If the city had forgotten my name, maybe I could remind it. Not by shouting—I suspected it didn’t respond to volume—but by doing the kinds of things that once taught it who I was.

I picked up a piece of trash that wasn’t mine and carried it three blocks until I found a bin. I gave directions to a tourist, even though I wasn’t entirely sure I was right. I held the subway door for a woman with too many bags, and when she said thank you, I said, “Of course,” like it was the most natural exchange in the world.

At first, nothing happened. The streetlights stayed indifferent, the trains arrived with mechanical disinterest.

Then, on the platform at Canal Street, a busker began to play a song I hadn’t heard in years—the one my father used to hum while fixing things around the house. I hadn’t thought of it in a long time. I dropped a dollar into the open case, and the musician looked up, really looked at me, as if trying to place a familiar face.

“Hey,” he said. “Do I know you?”

“Maybe,” I said. “I’m working on that.”

He nodded, like this made sense.

Further uptown, a stray cat brushed against my leg and didn’t flinch when I reached down. Its fur was warm, alive. It purred in a way that suggested recognition, or at least acceptance.

“Thanks,” I told it, and felt slightly ridiculous.

Around midnight, I found myself back at Delancey and Norfolk. The same corner. The same crossing. The same impatient taxis.

I waited.

The signal chirped. For a moment, I thought I heard it—something just beneath the electronic tone, a hint of a syllable shaped like me. Then a horn blared, and the moment snapped.

“You may cross,” the voice said.

I didn’t move.

A man beside me shifted his weight. “You going or what?”

“In a second.”

The light began to change. The window was closing. I stepped off the curb anyway, against the signal, against the rules that I had followed so precisely for so long.

Halfway across, a taxi barreled toward me, too fast, too close. I could see the driver’s face—tired, irritated, utterly unconcerned with my existence.

“Hey!” someone shouted.

Time did not slow. It did not stretch or bend or offer me a cinematic mercy. It simply continued, as it always does.

But something else changed.

The wind—always moving, always indifferent—paused.

Not for long. Just enough.

The taxi’s path shifted by an inch, then another, as if reconsidering. The driver’s eyes flickered, a microsecond of awareness sparking where there had been none.

I felt it then: a faint, fragile alignment. Not the full, comfortable recognition I had known before, but a beginning. A thread being tied.

The cab missed me by a safe margin. Not generous, not dramatic, but sufficient.

On the other side of the street, I exhaled.

The crossing signal chirped again, resetting for the next cycle. And in the space between tones, I almost heard it—my name, or the outline of it, returning.

Not given freely. Not restored all at once.

But remembered, piece by piece, as I remembered how to belong.
:::writing

:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
